tikz-graphtheory-dor1-01-f7587543: Undirected graph G with 18 vertices labelled A through R, drawn in a roughly grid-like layout. Edges connect adjacent letters plus several long-range pairs (e.g., F-N, K-P, L-R), illustrating the simplified street-map graph from the housing development.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-02-2a28815b: The same 18-vertex graph G (vertices A through R) redrawn with vertices scattered in a non-grid layout. The edge set is identical to the previous figure, illustrating that a graph's structure is independent of how it is drawn.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-03-108170f5: Schematic of Konigsberg's four landmasses drawn as labelled ellipses (North Bank, Island, East Bank, South Bank) with seven short line segments crossing between them representing the seven bridges of the puzzle.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-04-dd158e0f: Graph abstraction of the Konigsberg bridges: four circular vertices labelled NB, I, EB, and SB with seven edges connecting them, including two parallel arcs between NB and I and two between I and SB to represent the duplicate bridges (a multigraph).
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-05-b9d29786: Small graph illustrating a loop: three filled-dot vertices form a triangle and a red self-loop attaches to the rightmost vertex, showing an edge that connects a vertex to itself.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-06-02dc636b: Single isolated vertex with no edges, illustrating a vertex of degree 0 in the table of degree examples.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-07-3676507b: A vertex with one edge attached, illustrating degree 1 in the table of degree examples.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-08-09c16e85: A vertex with two edges attached (forming a V), illustrating degree 2 in the table of degree examples.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-09-91936202: A vertex with three edges attached, illustrating degree 3 in the table of degree examples.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-10-6fcd5b8a: A vertex with four edges attached (one to each diagonal direction), illustrating degree 4 in the table of degree examples.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-11-f0f82063: A 3-by-4 grid graph with 12 vertices labelled A-D (top), E-H (middle), J-M (bottom). All grid edges are drawn; a path from A to M is highlighted in thick red, traversing A-E-F-G-L-M.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-12-15aee7fd: The same 3-by-4 grid graph (vertices A-D, E-H, J-M) with a circuit highlighted in thick red, beginning and ending at vertex A by traversing A-E-J-K-F-B-A and similar back path, illustrating a cycle.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-13-f3b42ca4: A 3-by-4 grid graph with vertices A-D, E-H, J-M, but with the column-A horizontal edges deleted so that vertex A and the left column are isolated from the rest, illustrating a disconnected graph.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-14-644f57fa: Weighted graph of five U.S. cities (LA, Seattle, Chicago, Atlanta, Dallas) shown as filled dots arranged across the plane, with all ten edges drawn and labelled with one-way airfares such as $70 (LA-Seattle), $150 (LA-Dallas), $85 (Atlanta-Dallas), $75 (Chicago-Atlanta).
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-15-2fbde9e7: Initial-state diagram for Dijkstra's algorithm: seven blue circular nodes T, E, A, NB, MR, P, Y connected by weighted edges (e.g., T-A=20, A-NB=36, NB-Y=120, MR-Y=90); each node has a green-boxed tentative-distance label, all set to infinity except the destination T which is 0.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-16-ab330718: Weighted road network for the Yakima-to-Tacoma example: seven filled-dot vertices T (Tacoma), E (Eatonville), A (Auburn), NB (North Bend), MR (Mount Rainier), P (Packwood), Y (Yakima) with edges labelled by travel times in minutes (e.g., T-A=20, A-NB=36, NB-Y=104, MR-Y=96, P-Y=76).
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-17-a575d5d4: Yakima-to-Tacoma road network as before, now with the destination Y annotated [0] in red to mark it as the starting vertex of Dijkstra's algorithm with tentative distance zero.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-18-1f0da575: Yakima example after Dijkstra step 2: tentative distances [104] at NB, [96] at MR, [76] at P are recorded in red next to each neighbour of Y; Y still labelled [0]; remaining vertices T, E, A are unlabelled.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-19-ba0daf14: Yakima example after the second iteration: P is the current vertex; E now has tentative distance [172] (via P), and Y is shown struck-through (visited); other labels [104] at NB, [96] at MR, [76] at P remain.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-20-ad25bc85: Yakima example after the third iteration: A has tentative distance [175] (via MR); P and Y appear struck-through (visited); NB still [104], MR [96], E [172].
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-21-13989287: Yakima example after the fourth iteration: A's distance is updated to [140] via NB; NB, MR, P, Y all struck-through; E still [172].
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-22-a727336c: Yakima example after the fifth iteration: T receives tentative distance [160] via A; A, NB, MR, P, Y are struck-through, leaving E [172] still active. The shortest path Y-NB-A-T of 160 minutes is now determined.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-23-3c5cd5d8: A 4-vertex spanning tree drawn as filled dots at corners of a small region, connected by three edges forming a Y-shape (top-left to top vertex, bottom-right to middle, top to bottom-right), illustrating one possible spanning tree on four vertices.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-24-0b64247b: A 4-vertex spanning tree with three edges arranged as a path: middle vertex linked to top-right, top-right linked to bottom-right, and bottom-right linked to bottom-left, showing another spanning-tree configuration on four vertices.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-25-660dc0ab: Composite figure of five small spanning trees with n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 vertices arranged left-to-right; each tree has exactly n-1 edges, demonstrating that a spanning tree on n vertices uses n-1 edges.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-26-0e3f1efc: n = 3 case from the spanning-tree examples: three labelled vertices A, B, C connected by two edges (A-B and B-C) forming a path, with exactly n-1 = 2 edges.
tikz-graphtheory-dor1-27-35621dec: n = 4 case from the spanning-tree examples: a star tree with center B connected to leaves A, C, and D by three edges, illustrating n-1 = 3 edges on n = 4 vertices.
